That's because they play a crucial role in your blood circulation.

You see, your real heart sends blood all over your body, including your feet.

Getting the blood to go down to our feet is easy.

It's like sliding down a slide.

But getting it back up, it's like climbing back up the slide.

Your heart can't pull all the blood back up.

And that's where the calf muscles help with this big job.

When your calf muscles contract, they compress the vein valves in your legs and push the blood from your feet back up toward your heart.

That's why we call the calf muscles the second heart.

They single-handedly maintain normal blood circulation in your lower body.

Now here's the problem that I don't see anyone talking about.

As you sit for long hours every day and continue to age and become less physically active, your calf muscles get weaker.

As they get weaker, they can't contract as they used to.

So the vein valves in your calf muscles are responsible for pushing the blood back up to your heart, and those weaken as well.

When that happens, your calves can no longer send the old stale blood from your lower body back to your heart.

This buildup of old stale blood can cause swelling in your feet and ankles, but it can also lead to more serious foot conditions if left untreated.
